wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The two records describe individuals with completely different professional identities. The card describes a technical consultant and scientific journalist/editor specialized in film technology and industrial design, whereas the Wikidata entry describes a high-ranking Roman Catholic clergyman (Bishop). While they share the name William Warfel, their career paths are mutually exclusive.

results.possibleIdentity: William W. Warfel was a technical consultant, scientific journalist, and editor based in Oakville, California, who was active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He specialized in the technical fields of graphic arts, paper, film technology, photography, and industrial design. Warfel was a significant contributor to trade and technical publications in the printing industry, serving as an editor or frequent author for journals such as "Graphic Arts Monthly" and "The Inland Printer/American Lithographer." In 1970, he notably wrote about the implications of the metric system for the graphic arts industry. He was associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit that facilitated collaborations between the artistic and technical communities; his professional profile was maintained in the E.A.T. engineer and scientist directory to assist artists with expertise in graphics and film technology. Records indicate a William Wallace Warfel (1910–1993) resided in the Napa Valley area, which includes Oakville.
